narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model, trained on data from thousands of past students, estimated a 99.2% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first try—a risk too high for admission under our guidelines. This prediction stems directly from your academic record, particularly your placement in the second decile for grades in both your first and third undergraduate years, which lags well behind the top-decile performance typical of students who go on to pass the bar. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction was your third-year decile ranking, as it signaled a consistent struggle to compete academically compared to successful bar takers who averaged around the sixth decile. Your first-year decile came in as the second-strongest influence, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum that admitted students usually demonstrate much more strongly. Even your upper-middle family income bracket, while common among passers (making up about 45% of them), played a supporting role in tipping the scales toward failure rather than offsetting these academic gaps. Your LSAT score of 34 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90, both below the averages of 37.27 and 3.24 for those who passed, added to the picture but weren't the top drivers here. Being a full-time student aligns with the vast majority of successful candidates, so that didn't hurt your case. Overall, your profile stood out as weaker in core academic deciles than the typical admitted student, whose stronger year-by-year rankings predict bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from real outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters emphasizing your resilience and growth. Reapplying after addressing these key academic areas could shift the prediction in your favor, as many students improve dramatically with targeted effort. We're rooting for your success and encourage you to explore these steps.
